name: Mezz Mezzrow
abstract: Milton Mesirow (November 9, 1899 – August 5, 1972), better known as Mezz Mezzrow, was an American jazz clarinetist and saxophonist from Chicago, Illinois. He is remembered for organizing and financing recording sessions with Tommy Ladnier and Sidney Bechet.

about_quotes.speaker: Sammy Price
about_quotes.evidence: Milton and I became friends, very close friends, when he came home from being away in the '40s.

about_quotes.speaker: Big Chielf Moore
about_quotes.evidence: I was invited by Mezz Mezzrow along with Red Richards... all of us got a contract to go back to Paris for a tour in Europe. It was Mezz Mezzrow's project and we rehearsed and we played many of the principal cities

about_quotes.speaker: Bud Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: So, Floyd O'Brien and I got on a bus... and came to join Mezzrow... we were just rehearsing, and Mezzrow was feeding us... the thing didn't take

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: Mezz Mezzrow, who was not very active then and who had already gone to France and briefly come back from there – of course I read Mezz’s book, Really the Blues. That was another influential work in my jazz education. But I had never seen Mezz.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Freddie Moore
about_quotes.evidence: I went over there with Mezzrow... We had Jimmy Archey, myself, Pops Foster, Mezzrow, Lee Collins... And Mezzrow could transport [sic] all we didn't understand... Mezzrow would transport [sic] it, you know, from him to us.

about_quotes.speaker: Gordon Lorraine
about_quotes.evidence: And Mezz Mezzrow. You don't remember Mezz Mezzrow. Well, Johnnie Mae Mezzrow I think was Alfred’s girlfriend before I came along... She had a little son, this big, from Mezz Mezzrow—Mess, Junior. I used to love that boy.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gordon Lorraine — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
